content:
The **ROC curve** originated in **signal detection theory** during **World War II**, when engineers analyzed radar and sonar operators' ability to distinguish enemy targets from noise. The **Receiver Operating Characteristic** plotted hit rate against false-alarm rate as operator sensitivity changed — the same **TPR vs FPR** trade-off modern ML plots by sweeping a classifier threshold.

**Peterson**, **Birdsall**, and **Wald** formalized the framework in the **1950s**; **Green** and **Swets** (*Signal Detection Theory and Psychophysics*, 1966) brought it to psychology and medicine for diagnostic testing.

**Hanley** and **McNeil** (1982) popularized **AUC** for comparing medical diagnostic tests and clarified the **probabilistic interpretation** — AUC as the chance a diseased case ranks above a healthy one.

Machine learning adopted ROC-AUC in the **1990s–2000s** as probabilistic classifiers (logistic regression, SVMs, boosting) became standard. Libraries like **ROCR**, **pROC**, and **scikit-learn** made **roc_curve** and **roc_auc_score** default benchmarking tools — carrying wartime detection theory into modern tabular and deep binary heads.

timeline.label: WWII signal detection (1940s)
timeline.description: Radar/sonar analysts trade hit rate vs false alarms — precursor to TPR/FPR curves.

timeline.label: Formal ROC framework (1950s–60s)
timeline.description: Peterson & Birdsall, then Green & Swets, define ROC analysis for detection and psychophysics.

timeline.label: AUC for diagnostic tests (1982)
timeline.description: Hanley & McNeil establish AUC and its probabilistic interpretation for medical test comparison.

timeline.label: ML benchmarking standard (2000s–present)
timeline.description: ROC-AUC becomes default validation metric for binary classifiers in sklearn and production ML pipelines.
